data.title: EQT's Fund to Buy Crown Castle's Small Cells Solutions Business in $4.3 Billion Deal
data.description:
Swedish private equity company EQT agreed to buy US-listed Crown Castle's Small Cells Solutions business for a valuation of $4.25 billion, according to a Thursday release.

The EQT Active Core Infrastructure fund will acquire Small Cells Solutions, while EQT Infrastructure IV fund and Digital Bridge-backed Zayo will buy Crown Castle's Fiber Solutions business. The total valuation of the transactions is $8.5 billion.

Small Cells Solutions builds and operates digital infrastructure for the deployment of small cell networks to boost wireless connectivity.

EQT said the acquisition is expected to be completed in the first half of 2026, subject to regulatory and other customary approvals.

data.title: Acer Board OKs Up to NT$10 Billion Bond Issue
data.description:
Acer's board has approved the issuance of unsecured corporate bonds worth up to NT$10 billion, according to a Thursday Taiwan Exchange filing.

The bonds, to be issued at face value with a fixed coupon rate, will have a maturity of up to 10 years.

Proceeds will be used for debt repayment and working capital replenishment.

data.title: Genus Power Infrastructures Arm Acquires Remaining 13% Stake in Durg Rajnandgaon
data.description:
Genus Power Solutions, a wholly-owned subsidiary of Genus Power Infrastructures , has agreed to acquire the remaining 13% stake it does not own in Durg Rajnandgaon Jagdalpur Smart Metering, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

On completion of the transaction, Durg Rajnandgaon will become a wholly-owned subsidiary of Genus Power Solutions and a wholly owned step-down subsidiary of the company.

data.title: California Brands Are Leveraging the Latest E-Commerce Innovations to Scale Online Growth-Insights from Digital Silk
data.description:
Los Angeles, California--(Newsfile Corp. - March 14, 2025) - Digital Silk, an award-winning agency focused on creating brand strategies, custom websites, and digital marketing campaigns, is helping California brands maximize the latest e-commerce advancements to drive success.

With new AI-powered tools, enhanced customization options, and streamlined checkout experiences, businesses in California's thriving online retail sector are leveraging cutting-edge technology to scale and improve conversions.

California Brands Are Transforming E-Commerce with AI and UX Innovations-A Digital Silk Perspective

To view an enhanced version of this graphic, please visit:

https://images.newsfilecorp.com/files/10732/244472_04f71d9f3428f98e_001full.jpg

How California Brands Are Driving E-Commerce Growth

According to Statista, U.S. e-commerce sales are projected to surpass $1.3 trillion by 2025, fueled by digital transformation and increased consumer demand for seamless shopping experiences. California, a hub for digital-first brands, is at the forefront of adopting innovative solutions to enhance customer engagement and drive revenue growth.

Innovations Powering the Next Generation of Online Retail

From AI-powered product recommendations to frictionless checkout experiences, businesses are integrating cutting-edge technologies to optimize their online stores. Some of the key innovations reshaping the e-commerce landscape include:

  • AI-Driven Personalization - Brands are using artificial intelligence to create dynamic product recommendations, automate customer support, and enhance user experiences.

  • Optimized Checkout Experiences - Businesses are streamlining the checkout process to reduce cart abandonment and increase conversion rates.

  • Localized E-Commerce Expansion - Companies expanding internationally are adopting built-in localization tools to maintain compliance with regional regulations while reaching global audiences.

  • Hybrid Commerce Solutions - As online and offline shopping experiences blend, businesses are adopting integrated systems to create seamless omnichannel customer journeys.

Insights from Digital Silk on E-Commerce Growth

As a leader in e-commerce website development, Digital Silk has been at the forefront of helping brands implement the latest digital solutions to optimize performance. California-based businesses are seeing significant improvements in customer retention, faster load speeds, and increased mobile conversions through custom storefront designs and strategic UX/UI enhancements.

"Innovative digital solutions are game-changers for e-commerce brands looking to scale. Features like AI-driven personalization and optimized checkout experiences are reducing friction and driving higher conversions. At Digital Silk, we focus on integrating these advancements into tailored e-commerce solutions that help California brands streamline operations, boost customer retention, and maximize revenue potential." - says Ana Margarida Meira, Partner and Vice President of Client Relations at Digital Silk

California Brands Leading the Way

From direct-to-consumer fashion labels to tech accessory startups, California businesses are thriving by adopting the latest advancements in e-commerce. The integration of AI-driven insights, mobile-first design, and automation allows brands to offer seamless, high-converting shopping experiences.

As the digital commerce landscape continues to evolve, businesses that embrace these innovations will be best positioned for long-term success in an increasingly competitive online market.

About Digital Silk

Digital Silk is a full-service California e-commerce website design agency, dedicated to helping brands grow online. With a team of seasoned experts, Digital Silk delivers industry-leading digital experiences through strategic branding, cutting-edge web design, and digital marketing services that drive conversions and engagement.

Media Contact

Jessica Erasmus

Marketing Director & PR Manager

Tel: (800) 206-9413

Email: jessica@digitalsilk.com

To view the source version of this press release, please visit https://www.newsfilecorp.com/release/244472

data.title: NexGen Energy's Canada Project Timeline Delays May Boost Uranium Sector Sentiment, Euroz Hartley Says
data.description:
Delays to NexGen Energy's (ASX: NXG) Rook I Project timeline in Canada may boost the uranium sector, as concerns about the project's impact on the market may be overblown due to expected uranium supply shortages by the end of the decade, according to a Thursday note by Euroz Hartleys.

On Wednesday, the company said that the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission (CNSC) scheduled hearings for its Rook I Project in Canada for Nov. 19 and Feb. 9 to 13, 2026. It is currently evaluating the implications of the scheduled timeline for the project.

Euroz notes that the proposed federal hearing dates for Rook I have missed expectations of final federal approvals of the project in the first half of the year.

The 42-month construction period at the project can start once final permits are secured following the conclusion of a CNSC's hearing, Euroz added.

The firm now expects initial production at the project to begin in the first half of fiscal 2030, following revised expectations of final approvals being received in the March quarter of fiscal 2026.

Euroz Hartleys maintained Nexgen Energy's speculative buy rating but lowered its price target to AU$13.40 from AU$14.

Shares of the company rose 2% on market close.

data.title: Oasis: Condemns DIC's Decision To Relocate Some Of Its Most Valuable Art Works To The Facilities Of International House Of Japan
data.description:

data.title: Trump Doesn't Own This Market or This Economy — Yet. But He Will. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Andy Serwer

Ninety-two years ago this month — Saturday, March 4, 1933, to be exact — Franklin Delano Roosevelt, one hand on his family's age-worn Dutch bible opened to 1 Corinthians 13, was sworn in as the nation's 32nd president. In his inauguration speech, Roosevelt sought to rally our then-beleaguered nation, famously declaring (some say inspired by Thoreau) that "the only thing we have to fear is fear itself."

If our current commander in chief were to deliver a similar address today, he might candidly say that "the only thing certain is uncertainty itself."

There's much more, though, to contrast what President Donald Trump seems to be facilitating — inadvertently or otherwise — with what FDR had in mind, beyond that timeless catchphrase. FDR, both sides of the aisle would agree, ushered in an era of big government, which while some see as evil incarnate and others celebrate, has more or less continued unabated even through the Reagan years to the present. Now Trump is giving Roosevelt a run for his money as the most impactful president of the past century, albeit through a diametrically opposed strategy of shrinking the government.

With Elon Musk as his DOGEy wingman, Donald Trump is slashing government spending, mostly by laying off tens of thousands of government workers (out of a total three million federal employees). This, while presumably leaving most of the 43.5% of the government spending on entitlements — Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid — untouched. (Musk suggested this past week that Social Security might be on the chopping block, but the administration quickly walked that back.)

Will a lean and mean government, which Shark Tank investor Kevin O'Leary (aka "Mr. Wonderful") described gustatorily on Monday as currently "dripping with fat," function better? Heaven knows.

Even weightier unknowns this past week concerned Trump's tariffs against Mexico, Canada, China, and Europe, and retaliatory tariffs that have been announced, rescinded, and then reannounced faster and more furiously than the action in a Vin Diesel movie.

Then there's the flurry of executive orders flying out of the Oval Office, 89 as of last count, far surpassing the rate of any recent president during their first weeks in office. And yes, we're barely past the midpoint of Trump's first 100 days (we'll hit that on April 30), which might seem way longer to both his supporters and detractors. In case you missed marking that halfway point this past Tuesday, the White House celebrated the occasion with 50 wins in 50 days.

All of this is a lot — too much, it seems, for some market participants, particularly as the pace of Trump's rollout has picked up in recent weeks. "Business was really quite thrilled to have the idea of tax cuts and less regulation and with the idea that tariffs were a negotiating position," says Stephen Dover, chief market strategist at Franklin Templeton. "Now the market is sensing that Trump actually is going to have tariffs, and I don't think that was priced in."

And so, March has come in like an angry lion, with the S&P 500 index and Nasdaq Composite down 7.3% and 8.2% for the month, respectively, despite Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent saying on Thursday that he was "not concerned with a little volatility over the last three weeks." The selloff is fitting, perhaps, with the Ides of March looming this Saturday. Today, what's meant by the Trump market is markedly different from what the notion of a Trump market suggested back in those heady days of December. It's enough to make a billionaire wax existential: "We are born alone; we die alone and we navigate the Trump stock market alone," Third Point's Dan Loeb posted on X. Going out of March lamblike now seems aspirational.

The economy, too, is looking a bit peaked, or worse, showing signs the dreaded two-headed hydra of stagflation will emerge from its antediluvian slumber. Michael Gapen, chief U.S. economist at Morgan Stanley, recently lowered his gross-domestic-product estimates to 1.5% for 2025 and 1.2% in 2026, down from 1.9% and 1.3%, respectively, and raised his 2025 inflation forecast by 20 basis points to 2.5%. The firm's economists "expect earlier, broader tariffs to translate into softer growth earlier than previously anticipated" and "higher inflation in 2025 with a more pronounced and sooner reacceleration in prices." (A basis point is 1/100th of a percentage point.)

But really, how much of the market maelstrom and economic tepidness is Trump's doing?

Looking at stocks, it's remarkably underappreciated how high valuations have been recently. Robert Shiller's cyclically adjusted price/earnings, or CAPE, ratio, which uses a 10-year average of inflation-adjusted earnings to value stocks, was at 37.74 in January, the third-highest level in 100 years, loftier than in 1929. Stocks declined sharply shortly after each of these peaks.

As for the economy, wasn't inflation awakened in part by Joe Biden's stimulus programs? And when it comes to growth slowing, leaving aside the Covid mini-recession, we haven't had a real downturn since the 2008-09 financial crisis some 16 years ago, way outside the historical recession-every-six-or-seven-years cadence. One could argue we are simply due for a cyclical downturn.

All of which raises a couple of intriguing questions: 1) At what point do presidents deserve credit and/or blame for the stock market and economy after entering office, and 2) How much influence does a president have, anyway?

I reached out to Trump, as well as former Presidents Bush, Obama, and Biden, but didn't hear back. A representative for Clinton told me he couldn't be reached before my deadline.

Mr. Market, who presides over that most forward-looking of mechanisms, certainly doesn't wait for Inauguration Day to weigh in. "When Trump was elected last November, the market moved up because people thought he would be better for parts of the economy and Wall Street than Biden and the Democrats," says Richard Sylla, professor emeritus of economics at the NYU Stern School of Business. "It's been reversed a bit lately." Indeed, professor.

Keith Lerner, co-chief investment officer and chief market strategist at Truist, has charted the first year in the S&P 500 in each four-year presidential cycle since 1953. The data show the index rising on average about 1.3% into the first week of February, then declining about 1.8% into the first week of March, with continued choppiness until late April as investors digest new policies, before improving in May. This time around, "with the feverish pace of activity in Washington, the current cycle has been amplified," he says.

Lerner says there's another facet of the economy that reflects who's in the White House. "I think the current administration already owns trends in sentiment," he says. "With the economy it's hard to say, 'Hey, this is the day that they own it.' But consumer sentiment — that changes with the current administration. This is when they own it."

As far as when the White House should be held responsible for the overall economy — and specifically GDP growth — economist Allison Schrager, senior fellow at the Manhattan Institute, a conservative think tank, notes that "the Trump administration has said six months, so I would say after the second quarter. But we could be generous and give them until the third."

Schrager is referring to an interview with Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, in which he acknowledged that over the next six to 12 months, Trump will be responsible for the economy. For now, though, Bessent says, we're "still living through this 'Bidenflation,' " which the consumer price index for February suggests may be easing. Consumer prices rose by 2.8% versus an expected 2.9% year over year, and 0.2% versus an expected 0.3% for the month.

Still, the reaction was muted. Economists think the impact of tariffs has yet to be reflected — and unlikely to spur any rate-cut relief. "Equities are unlikely to get into full Fed put glee mode," Seema Shah, chief global strategist at Principal Asset Management, said in a note to clients. "It's worth remembering that this may be the calm CPI report before the storm."

But how much sway does a president really hold? "I think both the public as well as market participants overestimate a president's impact," says Franklin Templeton's Dover. "We are in an economy that's largely run by the private sector, and valuations are primarily based on interest rates and profits." He acknowledges, though, that Trump, particularly with his use of executive orders, is different.

Veteran Washington-watcher Greg Valliere writes that Trump may be acting strategically here. In a thought-provoking piece titled "When to Take a Recession?" Valliere wonders if Trump is intentionally sandbagging the economy in order to play the hero later, perhaps by cutting taxes. "Even if there's no recession — and there may not be one — Trump can take credit late this year for avoiding one. It's a win-win for the president, who has cleverly set the stage for a recession that, he will argue, only he can avoid."

All of this would be lost on our grandparents. "Before the 1930s, people didn't pay that much attention to what presidents were doing," says Sylla. "FDR was the beginning of what some call 'the imperial presidency.' "

Which brings us back to FDR and 1 Corinthians 13, and one of its most famous passages: "For now we see through a glass, darkly; but then face to face: now I know in part; but then shall I know even as also I am known," which means, in part, that now we see things imperfectly, but in the future we will see more clearly and with greater understanding.

Here's to hoping.

Write to Andy Serwer at andy.serwer@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: FDE Secures EUR10 Million in Loans from Bpifrance
data.description:
La Française de l'Energie , d/b/a FDE, said Thursday it secured two loans totaling 10 million euros from public investment bank Bpifrance.

The funding includes a 10-year Prêt Croissance Relance loan to support its French and international expansion plans beyond its current projects and the five-year Prêt Vert loan, which is 50% guaranteed by the national Fonds Prêt Vert 4 fund under the Plan France Relance.

The French energy producer said the Prêt Vert funding is allocated specifically to strengthen its environmental initiatives and support its goal of preventing over 20 million tons of carbon dioxide emissions annually by 2030.

data.title: Market Chatter: AB InBev Denies Job Cuts at Budweiser APAC
data.description:
Anheuser-Busch InBev denied its Asia-Pacific unit Budweiser Brewing APAC is planning to reduce its workforce by the thousands in 2025, Reuters reported Thursday.

"We will continue our long-term investment in China," Reuters quoted an AB InBev spokesperson as saying.

The brewery will cut its operational expenses by about 15% in 2025, including thousands of job cuts, in addition to a 16% reduction of its 25,000-strong workforce in 2024, Bloomberg News reported separately.

The cuts would mostly come from China where most of its workforce is located, according to Bloomberg.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: FY Group to Issue NT$650 Million Bonds; Shares Drop 6%
data.description:
FY Group approved its first issuance of unsecured convertible bonds in Taiwan worth NT$650 million.

The five-year bonds will be offered at 100% to 101% of par value with a 0% coupon rate.

Proceeds will fund factory expansion, new machinery, and working capital, it added.

Shares of the furniture manufacturer fell 6% recently.

data.title: Afya Ltd reports results for the quarter ended December 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Afya Ltd reported quarterly adjusted earnings of R$2.10​​ per share for the quarter ended December 31, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of R$1.79. The mean expectation of five analysts for the quarter was for earnings of R$1.69 per share. Wall Street expected results to range from R$1.34 to R$1.97 per share.

  • Revenue rose 16.3% to R$849.02 million from a year ago; analysts expected R$849.90 million.

  • Afya Ltd's reported EPS for the quarter was R$1.65​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of R$149.93 million.

  • Afya Ltd shares had risen by 2.1% this quarter.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 0.5% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is 3 "strong buy" or "buy," 2 "hold" and 3 "sell" or "strong sell."

  • The average consensus recommendation for the schools, colleges & universities peer group is also "hold"

Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Afya Ltd is $17.50

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data March 14 at 05:21 a.m. UTC. ​All figures in Brazilian reais unless otherwise stated. (For questions concerning the data in this report, contact Estimates.Support@lseg.com. For any other questions or feedback, contact RefinitivNewsSupport@thomsonreuters.com)

data.title: Air Liquide Launches EUR500 Million Green Bond Issue
data.description:
Air Liquide , via its unit Air Liquide Finance, issued a new green bond worth 500 million euros, with an annual coupon of 3.5% and maturity on March 21, 2035.

The French industrial gases company will use the proceeds from the issue to finance and refinance its energy transition projects in the field of low-carbon hydrogen and air gases, according to a Thursday release.

The offer's settlement will take place on March 21.

data.title: HMM May Be Less Affected Than Peers by Potential U.S. Regulations on China — Market Talk
data.description: HMM may be less affected than its local peers by potential U.S. regulations on China, Nomura analyst Eon Hwang writes in a note. The South Korean shipping company operates a fleet of 125 vessels globally, and only 17 of them are Chinese-built ones, possibly subject to U.S. sanctions, Hwang says. If the U.S. were to hit Chinese vessels with high additional port fees, HMM could sell Chinese-built ships, as U.S. shipping routes account for 40% of its revenue, he adds. Nomura upgrades its rating on the stock to buy from neutral and raises the target price by 47% to KRW25,000. Shares are 0.2% higher at KRW20,650. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: Kirloskar Brothers Names General Manager, Head of Legal
data.description:
Pump manufacturing company Kirloskar Brothers' appointed Amit Oswal as general manager and head of legal, effective March 13, according to a same-day Indian bourse filing.

Oswal replaces Subodh Karmarkar who resigned, effective March 12.

data.title: Carnegie Access: Spago Nanomedical: Highlights from Carnegie’s Healthcare Conference
data.description:
Carnegie has published a new flash comment on Spago Nanomedical following recent news flow.

To access the full report and subscribe to future updates, visit https://www.carnegie.se/en/commissioned-research/spago/

data.title: KH Group Explores Options for Indoor Group Stake
data.description:
KH Group said Thursday it was exploring options, including a sale, for its 58.3% owned home furnishings and interior decoration products retailer Indoor Group.

The Nordic conglomerate hired a financial advisor and aims to complete the process within the year. It noted that there was no certainty regarding the timing, terms, and completion of a potential transaction.

The group launched the review as part of its strategy to focus on its KH-Koneet business, which involves exiting other business areas.

data.title: Pertama Digital Enters Deal To Purchase 80% Stake In D-Ron SG And D-Ron MY
data.description:
Pertama Digital Bhd :

  • DEAL TO PURCHASE 80% STAKE IN D-RON SG FOR SGD30.4 MILLION

  • ENTERED INTO A TERM SHEET WITH THREE INDIVIDUALS

  • DEAL FOR 5 MILLION RGT FOR 80% STAKE IN D RON MALAYSIA

data.title: CHN Energy to Offload Hubei Songzi Power Station for 361 Million Yuan
data.description:
CHN Energy Changyuan Electric Power agreed to offload the Hubei Songzi Pumped Storage Power Station for 361.3 million yuan, according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange disclosure on Friday.

Guoneng Hubei Songzi Pumped Storage, a subsidiary of National Energy Group Hubei Energy, will buy the power station, the Chinese electricity and heat provider said.

data.title: Estonian Lender Coop Pank Issues First International Covered Bonds
data.description:
Coop Pank (CPA1T.TL) said Thursday it completed its maiden issuance of international covered bonds worth 250 million euros, maturing in four years.

The bonds backed by Estonian residential mortgage loans are subject to a coupon rate of 3.125% and a re-offer yield of 3.147%.

Coop Pank issued the bonds to European institutional investors under its 750 million-euro covered bond program, which was established to diversify its funding structure.

data.title: Palm Oil Heads for Weekly Losses
data.description:
Malaysian palm oil futures were little changed, hovering around MYR 4,540 per tonne after a 1% surge in the previous session, as traders awaited export estimates from cargo surveyors for March 1–15, due over the weekend.

In February, industry data showed shipments fell 16.27% to a four-year low of 1 million tons.

Market participants also anticipated January-February activity data in key buyer China next week, notably industrial output and retail sales, which could signal future palm oil demand.

For the week, futures are down 1.8% so far, reversing prior gains amid concerns over the impact of US tariffs on the global trade.

These losses were cushioned, however, by a 35.7% surge in India’s February imports, likely due to stock replenishment and price advantages.

On the supply side, Malaysia's inventories fell for the fifth month to 1.51 million tons, the lowest since April 2023, while production dropped 4.16% to a three-year low of 1.19 million tons due to flood-related disruptions.

data.title: HMM Raised to Buy From Neutral by Nomura >011200.SE
TYC Brother Industrial Acquires Land in Taiwan for NT$194 Million
data.description:
TYC Brother Industrial acquired land in Tainan City, Taiwan for NT$193.7 million to build an automatic warehouse.

The 1,684-ping property was purchased from related parties, Briteview Automotive Lighting, and Buildup International Trading, according to a Thursday Taiwan Exchange filing.

The move aims to streamline warehouse management and support operational needs, it said.

data.title: New Zealand Stocks Snap 3-day Retreat
data.description:
New Zealand's benchmark S&P/NZX 50 index rose 0.5% to close at 12,266 on Friday, ending a three-day losing streak, amid strength in consumer non-durables and technology services sectors.

In economic news, New Zealand's manufacturing activity in February rose to its highest level since August 2022, driven by higher production and new orders.

Meanwhile, annual food inflation ticked up to 2.4% in February from 2.3% in the previous month.

Among individual stocks, dairy producer A2 Milk surged 7%, making it the session’s top performer, as dairy prices remained near record highs.

Additionally, aged-care providers Ryman Healthcare and Summerset Group gained 2.5% and 1.6%, respectively.

Despite Friday’s rebound, the index fell 1.1% for the week, marking its fourth consecutive weekly decline, amid mounting concerns over an escalating global trade war.

data.title: Wolters Kluwer Prices EUR500 Million Bonds
data.description:
Wolters Kluwer said Thursday it priced its offering of 500 million euros of 3.375% senior unsecured bonds due March 20, 2032.

The Dutch information services company will use the net proceeds for general corporate expenditures. Settlement is scheduled for March 20, 2025.

ABN AMRO, Bank of America, Citigroup, ING Bank, and Rabobank served as joint active bookrunners for the bonds that will be listed on the Luxembourg stock exchange.

data.title: Gospell Digital Tech's Unit Bags New Energy Power Project for 63 Million Yuan
data.description:
A consortium with Gospell Digital Technology's unit, Gaos Bell Digital Technology (Shandong), won the bid for a regional new energy power consumption platform construction project for 63.1 million yuan, according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange disclosure on Friday.

The project, which will include residential communities, businesses, schools, hospitals, and commercial centers, is located in Weifang City, China.

The construction will run within a year, the Chinese software and hardware products producer said.

Shandong Sanhe New Energy Technology and Shandong Guoneng Power Design are the other consortium members.

Gospell's shares rose over 2% in recent trade.

data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: Markets Remain on Edge as Trade War Heats Up
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

Germany CPI, balance of payments; UK GDP, trade, index of production; trading updates from Daimler Truck, BMW, Berkeley Group

Opening Call:

European stock futures rose early Friday as the threat of a U.S. government shutdown ebbed. Asian stock benchmarks advanced; the dollar and Treasury yields rose; while oil and gold futures also pushed higher.

Equities:

Stock futures point to a slightly higher open Friday, as relief over the likely aversion of a U.S. government shutdown supported sentiment.

An escalation in trade tensions spurred another rocky day on Wall Street overnight, with investors on edge over new tariff threats and mixed inflation news.

President Trump on Thursday opened a new front to the global trade war, threatening a 200% tariff on U.S. imports of all alcoholic beverages from the European Union.

"That pattern of announcements and imposition of tariffs in fairly rapid fashion is unsettling the markets," said Jason Pride, chief of investment strategy and research at Glenmede.

As trade tensions continue to rise, a big risk for the U.S. is that other countries get to a point where they have had enough and refuse to negotiate with Trump, said Shane Oliver, chief economist at AMP.

Forex:

Safe-haven currencies such as the dollar may continue to benefit from lingering risk aversion. More U.S. tariff headlines are weighing on sentiment, said NAB's Rodrigo Catril.

"Adding to the risk aversion in the air, Russian president flipped the U.S.-Ukraine proposal on its head saying you can't have an 'immediate' ceasefire without first agreeing on terms," he said. A search for safe-haven has been the overnight theme, Catril added.

Bonds:

Treasury yields fell overnight, chipping away at two straight days of gains. Investors on Thursday received relatively favorable U.S. inflation data for February.

"Of course, this is only one month - and doesn't yet include the impact of tariffs - so many people will discount these numbers. However, they do speak to the underlying trend pre-tariffs taking effect, and at least we are starting from a better place," said Chris Zaccarelli, chief investment officer for Northlight Asset Management.

That could give more breathing room to the Federal Reserve at its policy meeting next week.

Energy:

OPEC's decision to go ahead with its planned production hikes in April implies the oil market is entering a new phase where it lets underlying fundamentals drive prices, but the opposite might be true, said Daniel Hynes, senior commodity strategist at ANZ.

Political considerations may also have been behind the decision, he added. Trump hasn't been shy in asking OPEC to help keep oil prices lower. Any rise in OPEC supply could also help the U.S. put pressure on Iran and Venezuela, Hynes added.

Metals:

The value of gold has nearly doubled in the past five years, crossing the $3,000-an-ounce threshold for the first time ever late Thursday-which may signal a structural shift in the sector that may feed long-term strength.

"The gold market isn't just reacting to economic conditions, it's revealing deeper structural issues that could reshape the industry," with trust in paper gold markets "eroding," said Alex Ebkarian at Allegiance Gold.

"Gold is in a secular bull market and is ignoring possible headwinds from rising Treasury yields and short-term profit-taking," Ebkarian said. "Instead, it's responding to cumulative macroeconomic fears: declining consumer confidence, persistent inflation, expanding job cuts and geopolitical uncertainty around tariffs."

-

Base metals broadly advanced in Asian trading. Investors are gauging market appetite for new highs despite U.S. recession risks in focus amid renewed geopolitical tensions between Russia and Ukraine, Sucden Financial said.

The lack of fundamental and technical triggers mean a significant push higher in prices will be challenging, Sucden Financial said.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

Gold tops $3,000. Here's what investors joining the gold rush need to know.

The value of gold has nearly doubled in the past five years, crossing the $3,000-an-ounce threshold for the first time ever late Thursday - and the precious metal's latest move may be signaling a structural shift in the sector that may feed long-term strength.

"The gold market isn't just reacting to economic conditions, it's revealing deeper structural issues that could reshape the industry," with trust in paper gold markets "eroding," said Alex Ebkarian, chief operating officer and co-founder of precious-metals dealer Allegiance Gold.

Democrats Clear Way for GOP Funding Bill, Ending Threat of Government Shutdown

WASHINGTON-The leader of Senate Democrats moved to take the threat of a government shutdown off the table, following a grueling intraparty fight in which lawmakers struggled with how best to resist President Trump's fast-paced efforts to slim down federal agencies.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D., N.Y.) said he would vote to advance a Republican measure, set for Friday afternoon, to fund the government through the rest of the year. Earlier, in a closed-door lunch, he also said that enough Democrats would join him to help the Republicans clear the chamber's critical 60-vote hurdle, people familiar with the matter said.

Trump's Ukraine Peace Strategy Put to Test After Putin Balks at Cease-Fire

WASHINGTON-President Trump finds himself a "yes" away from brokering a cease-fire between Russia and Ukraine. But it is Russian President Vladimir Putin who has to say it, and he is leaning in the other direction.

That has put Trump, the self-professed master dealmaker, in a bind of his own making. After promising Putin wants peace and pressuring Kyiv to back the 30-day fighting pause, Trump will face a decision on imposing new sanctions on Moscow, as he has vowed to do if the Kremlin balks at the U.S.-brokered plan.

A New Hope for Europe's Ailing Economies: the Military

A massive military buildup across Europe could achieve what governments have failed to do in years: jump-start a sluggish economy, seed new innovations and create new industries.

Countries from the U.K. to Germany and Denmark have announced vast increases in military spending to counter Russia's threats as the U.S. warns Europe not to take America's protection for granted.

From Champagne to Bourbon, Alcohol Lands on Front Lines of Global Trade War

Alcohol has landed on the front lines of a global trade war, throwing French wines, Irish whiskeys, Kentucky bourbon, Japanese beer and Mexican tequila into the crossfire between the U.S. and its biggest trading partners.

In the Canadian province of Ontario, liquor stores have cleared their shelves of California wine and Tennessee whiskey, replacing them with local varieties and signs declaring: "For the good of Canada." A restaurant wine director in Washington state is dreading the arrival of shipping containers full of European wine so expensive it's unsellable. The Kentucky governor is pleading for the survival of his bourbon industry. And makers of American sparkling wines are poised to make a windfall.

Inside the Mind of Intel's New CEO: 'Disrupt and Leapfrog'

They say Lip-Bu Tan is the best hope to fix Intel-if Intel can be fixed at all.

Tan's selection as the chipmaker's new chief executive was cheered by analysts, bankers and shareholders. He brings two decades of semiconductor industry experience, relationships across the sector, a startup mindset and an obsession with AI...and basketball.

data.title: Indonesia issues telco permit for some of Apple's iPhone 16 series
data.description:
Indonesia's communications ministry has issued a telecommunications permit for some of Apple's iPhone 16 series, according to the ministry's website.

The issue came a week after Indonesia issued local content certificates for iPhone 16. Five certificates were issued for five different iPhone 16 models.

data.title: Neste Issues EUR700 Million Green Bond
data.description:
Neste said Thursday it issued 700 million euros worth of a 3.750% green bond with a five-year term.

The Finnish oil refiner and renewable fuel producer issued the bond under its euro medium-term note program, with proceeds earmarked for projects and assets eligible under its green finance framework.

Interim Chief Financial Officer Anssi Tammilehto added that net proceeds will be used for the Rotterdam refinery in the Netherlands, which is expected to be the largest renewable diesel and sustainable aviation fuel facility in the world.
